summary: This notebook provides a comprehensive exploratory data analysis (EDA) of the NFL Big Data Bowl 2025 dataset, systematically examining player demographics, game metadata, play-level statistics, and spatial tracking data. Through histograms, bar charts, scatter plots, and heatmaps, the author highlights key distributions such as player birth years, weights, and pre-snap win probabilities while contextualizing NFL-specific features like formations and pass results. The analysis reveals dataset characteristics, identifies potential data quirks like date parsing inconsistencies, and establishes a foundational understanding of the data's structure and spatial dynamics. Readers should gain clear insights into the dataset's statistical properties and spatial patterns to inform downstream predictive modeling efforts.
competition_flow: Loads raw NFL competition CSVs, performs extensive statistical and spatial EDA with visualizations, and documents dataset characteristics without training any models.
data_reading: pd.read_csv() for player_play.csv, games.csv, plays.csv, players.csv, and tracking_week_1.csv.
data_processing: Date parsing with pd.to_datetime(format='mixed') and DatetimeIndex extraction.
Filtering tracking data by playId, gameId, and playDirection.
Aggregation via pivot_table and value_counts for categorical distributions.
insights: Player birth years follow a left-skewed distribution with peaks around 1995-1996.
Player weights are approximately normally distributed with a mean of 246 lbs and median of 236 lbs.
Pre-snap home team win probability exhibits a normal, unimodal, and symmetric distribution.
Most NFL games occur on Sundays, typically kicking off at 1:00 PM ET.
Tracking data can be effectively visualized using 2D scatter plots and heatmaps to understand spatial player movements and tackle locations.
critical_findings: Some date columns in the dataset do not strictly follow the %Y-%m-%d format, requiring format='mixed' in pd.to_datetime().
The author notes confusion regarding the most frequent game time (13:00 ET) versus typical NFL scheduling, highlighting a potential data representation quirk.
